questions.question:
Suppose the predicate F(x, y, t) is used to represent the statement that person x can fool person y at time t. Which one of the statements below expresses best the
meaning of the formula ∀x∃y∃t(¬F(x, y,t))? [ MCQ || GATE 2010 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Suppose the predicate F(x, y, t) is used to represent the statement that person x can fool person y at time t. Which one of the statements below expresses best the

meaning of the formula ∀x∃y∃t(¬F(x, y,t))?[ MCQ || GATE 2010 || 2 Marks ]

options.text: Everyone can fool some person at some time
options.html: Everyone can fool some person at some time

options.text: No one can fool everyone all the time
options.html: No one can fool everyone all the time

options.text: Everyone cannot fool some person all the time
options.html: Everyone cannot fool some person all the time

options.text: No one can fool some person at some time
options.html: No one can fool some person at some time

questions.explanation:
F(x,y,t) ⇒ Person 'x' can fool person 'y' at time 't'.
Now we pull the negation sign by applying Demorgan's law.
∀x∃y∃t(¬F(x, y, t)) ≡ ¬∃x∀y∀t(F(x,y,t))
¬∃x∀y∀t(F(x,y,t)) ⇒ No person can fool all the person all the time.
Which is same as "No one can fool everyone all the time"
questions.explanation_html:
F(x,y,t) ⇒ Person 'x' can fool person 'y' at time 't'.

Now we pull the negation sign by applying Demorgan's law.

∀x∃y∃t(¬F(x, y, t)) ≡ ¬∃x∀y∀t(F(x,y,t))

¬∃x∀y∀t(F(x,y,t)) ⇒ No person can fool all the person all the time.

Which is same as "No one can fool everyone all the time"

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Graph Theory |Eulers Formula | PYQ | Question | 2012
questions.question: Let G be a simple undirected planar graph on 10 vertices with 15 edges. If G is a connected graph, then the number of bounded faces in any embedding of G on the plane is equal to ____. [ MCQ || GATE 2012 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: Let G be a simple undirected planar graph on 10 vertices with 15 edges. If G is a connected graph, then the number of bounded faces in any embedding of G on the plane is equal to ____. [ MCQ || GATE 2012 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
As the given graph is planar, then we have to consider Euler’s formula which is:
v-e+f=2
Given 10 vertices & 15 edges,so
10-15+f=2
f=2+15-10
f=7
There will be an unbounded face always.
So, the number of bounded faces = 6.

So, the number of bounded faces = 6.

questions.question: How many onto (or surjective) functions are there from an n-element (n ≥ 2) set to a 2-element set? [ MCQ || GATE 2012 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html: How many onto (or surjective) functions are there from an n-element (n ≥ 2) set to a 2-element set? [ MCQ || GATE 2012 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Onto function is possible if m ≥ n. So,
number of onto functions possible is:
n ^m  -  ^ n C _1 (n-1) ^ m  +  ^n C _2  (n-2) ^m  + .......
According to the Question:
m = n, n = 2
So, number of onto functions:
= 2 ^ n  -  ^ 2 C _ 1 (2-1) ^ n  + ^  2 C _ 2  (2-2) ^n
= 2 ^ n  - 2 × 1 + 0
= 2 ^ n  – 2

number of onto functions possible is:

m = n, n = 2

So, number of onto functions:

= 2 n  -  2 C 1 (2-1) n  +  2 C 2  (2-2) n

= 2 n  - 2 × 1 + 0

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for undirected graphs?
P: Number of odd degree vertices is even.
Q: Sum of degrees of all vertices is even. [ MCQ || GATE 2013 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE for undirected graphs?

P: Number of odd degree vertices is even.

questions.explanation:
P is true: For undirected graph as adding an edge always increases degree of two vertices by 1.

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Graph Theory |Line Graph | PYQ | Question | 2013
questions.question:
The line graph L(G) of a simple graph G is defined as follows:
There is exactly one vertex v(e) in L(G) for each edge e in G.
For any two edges e and e' in G, L(G) has an edge between v(e) and v(e'), if and only if e and e' are incident with the same vertex in G.
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
(P) The line graph of a cycle is a cycle.
(Q) The line graph of a clique is a clique.
(R) The line graph of a planar graph is planar.
(S) The line graph of a tree is a tree. [ MCQ || GATE 2013 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
The line graph L(G) of a simple graph G is defined as follows:

There is exactly one vertex v(e) in L(G) for each edge e in G.

For any two edges e and e' in G, L(G) has an edge between v(e) and v(e'), if and only if e and e' are incident with the same vertex in G.

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

(P) The line graph of a cycle is a cycle.

(Q) The line graph of a clique is a clique.

(R) The line graph of a planar graph is planar.

(S) The line graph of a tree is a tree. [ MCQ || GATE 2013 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
P) True.
Because every edge in the cycle graph will become a vertex in the new graph L(G) and every vertex of cycle graph will become an edge in the new graph.
R) False.
We can give an counter- example. Let a graph G has 5 vertices and 9 edges and it is planar. Assume degree of one vertex is 2 and of all others are 4. Now, L(G) has 9 vertices (because G has 9 edges) and 25 edges. But for a graph to be planar,
|E| ≤ 3|v| - 6
For 9 vertices |E| ≤ 3 × 9 - 6
⇒ |E| ≤ 27 - 6
⇒ |E| ≤ 21.
But L(G) has 25 edges and so is not planar.
As option (R) is false, option (B) & (C) are eliminated.
questions.explanation_html:

questions.question_html:
What is the logical translation of the following statement?

"None of my friends are perfect" [ MCQ || GATE 2013 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Let F(x) = x is my friend
P(x) = x is perfect
The meaning of ∃x(P(x) ∧ F(x)) is at least one person who is my friend and perfect.
The negation of ∃x(P(x)∧F(x)) is “This is not the case that at least one person who is my friend and perfect”.
So ~∃x(P(x)∧F(x)) means that “None of my friends are perfect”.

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Mathematical Logics | Equivalent | PYQ | Question | 2013
questions.question: Which one of the following is NOT logically equivalent to ¬∃x(∀y(α) ∧ ∀z(β)) ? [ MSQ || GATE 2013 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html: Which one of the following is NOT logically equivalent to ¬∃x(∀y(α) ∧ ∀z(β)) ? [ MSQ || GATE 2013 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Option A:
∀x(∃z(¬β) →∀y(α))
⇒∀x(¬∃z(¬β) ∨ ∀y(α))
⇒∀x(∀z(β) ∨ ∀y(α))
⇒¬∃x¬(∀z(β) ∨ ∀y(α))
⇒¬∃x(¬∀z(β)∧¬∀y(α))
A is not equivalent to the given.
Option B:
∀x(∀z(β)→∃y(¬α))
⇒∀x(¬∀z(β)∨∃y(¬α))
⇒¬∃x¬(¬∀z(β)∨∃y(¬α))
⇒¬∃x(∀z(β)∨∀y(α))
B is Matching and equivalent to given.
Option C:
∀x(∀y(α)→∃z(¬β))
⇒∀x(¬∀y(α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))
⇒¬∃x¬(¬∀y(α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))
⇒¬∃x(∀y(α)∧ ∀z(β))
C is equivalent to the given.
Option D:
∀x(∃y(¬α)→∃z(¬β))
⇒∀x(¬∃y(¬α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))
⇒∀x(∀y(α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))
⇒¬∃x¬(∀y(α)∨∃z(¬β))
⇒¬∃x(¬∀y(α)∧¬∃z(¬β))
⇒¬∃x(¬∀y(α)∧∀z(β))
So D is Not equivalent to the given.
questions.explanation_html:
Option A:

∀x(∃z(¬β) →∀y(α))

⇒∀x(¬∃z(¬β) ∨ ∀y(α))

⇒∀x(∀z(β) ∨ ∀y(α))

⇒¬∃x¬(∀z(β) ∨ ∀y(α))

⇒¬∃x(¬∀z(β)∧¬∀y(α))

A is not equivalent to the given.

Option B:

∀x(∀z(β)→∃y(¬α))

⇒∀x(¬∀z(β)∨∃y(¬α))

⇒¬∃x¬(¬∀z(β)∨∃y(¬α))

⇒¬∃x(∀z(β)∨∀y(α))

B is Matching and equivalent to given.

Option C:

∀x(∀y(α)→∃z(¬β))

⇒∀x(¬∀y(α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))

⇒¬∃x¬(¬∀y(α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))

⇒¬∃x(∀y(α)∧ ∀z(β))

C is equivalent to the given.

Option D:

∀x(∃y(¬α)→∃z(¬β))

⇒∀x(¬∃y(¬α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))

⇒∀x(∀y(α) ∨ ∃z(¬β))

⇒¬∃x¬(∀y(α)∨∃z(¬β))

⇒¬∃x(¬∀y(α)∧¬∃z(¬β))

⇒¬∃x(¬∀y(α)∧∀z(β))

So D is Not equivalent to the given.

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Set Theory | Properties | PYQ | Question | 2013
questions.question: A binary operation ⊕ on a set of integers is defined as x ⊕ y = x ^2  + y ^2 . Which one of the following statements is TRUE about ⊕ ? [ MCQ || GATE 2013 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: A binary operation ⊕ on a set of integers is defined as x ⊕ y = x 2  + y 2 . Which one of the following statements is TRUE about ⊕ ? [ MCQ || GATE 2013 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Commutative property:
A binary relation on a set S is called commutative if a*b = b*a ∀x, y ∈S.
Associative property:
A binary relation on set is called associative if (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) ∀x,y ∈ S.
Given x ⊕ y = x ^ 2  + y ^ 2  --------(1)
Replace x, y in (1):
y ⊕ x = y ^2  + x ^2  which is same as (1), so this
is commutative.
(x ⊕ y) ⊕z = (x ^2  + y ^ 2 ) ⊕ z
= (x ^ 2  + y ^ 2 ) ^2 + z ^2
= x ^2  + y ^ 2  + z ^2  + 2x ^ 2 y ^2  ----------(2)
x⊕(y ⊕ z) = x ⊕ (y ^2  + z ^ 2 )
= x ^2  + (y ^2  + z ^2 ) ^2
= x ^2  + y ^2  + z ^2  + 2y ^2 z ^2  ----------- (3)
(2) & (3) are not same, so this is not associative.
questions.explanation_html:
Commutative property:

A binary relation on a set S is called commutative if a*b = b*a ∀x, y ∈S.

Associative property:

A binary relation on set is called associative if (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) ∀x,y ∈ S.

Given x ⊕ y = x 2  + y 2  --------(1)

Replace x, y in (1):

y ⊕ x = y2  + x 2  which is same as (1), so this

is commutative.

(x ⊕ y) ⊕z = (x 2  + y 2 ) ⊕ z

= (x 2  + y 2 )2 + z 2

= x 2  + y 2  + z 2  + 2x 2 y2  ----------(2)

x⊕(y ⊕ z) = x ⊕ (y2  + z 2 )

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Graph Theory | isomorphic | PYQ | Question 2| 2014
questions.question: A cycle on n vertices is isomorphic to its complement. The value of n is _____. [ NAT || GATE 2014 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html: A cycle on n vertices is isomorphic to its complement. The value of n is _____.[ NAT || GATE 2014 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
According to Complement of a graph, we need to remove the existing edges from the graph and place the new edges which were not present in the actual graph.
In a cycle of n vertices, each vertex is connected to the other two vertices. So each vertex’s degree is 2.
When we complement it, each vertex will be connected to the remaining n-3 vertices(one is self and two other vertices in the actual graph).
As given in the question,
n-3 =2
n = 3 + 2
n = 5
Graph 1 and Graph 2 are complements of each other.
So, the value of n is 5.

Observe that in the given statements, "not cheap & cheap”, "good & not good" are used.

All L, M, N are true.

A pennant is a sequence of numbers, each number being 1 or 2. An n-pennant is a sequence of numbers with sum equal to n.
For example, (1,1,2) is a 4-pennant. The set of all possible 1-pennants is {(1)}, the set of all possible 2-pennants is {(2), (1,1)}and the set of all 3-pennants is {(2,1), (1,1,1), (1,2)}.

questions.question_html:
A pennant is a sequence of numbers, each number being 1 or 2. An n-pennant is a sequence of numbers with sum equal to n.

⇒ 9!/8!1! = 9 pennants

Three two’s: 2221111 ⇒ 7!/3!4! = 35
Four two’s: 222211 ⇒ 6!/4!2! = 15
Five two’s: 22222 ⇒ 1
Total = 89 pennants.

questions.question: Let G be a group with 15 elements. Let L be a subgroup of G. It is known that L ≠ G and that the size of L is at least 4. The size of L is __________. [NAT || GATE 2014 Set-3 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: Let G be a group with 15 elements. Let L be a subgroup of G. It is known that L ≠ G and that the size of L is at least 4. The size of L is __________. [NAT || GATE 2014 Set-3 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
According to Lagrange's theorem, in the mathematics of group theory, for any finite group G, the order (number of elements) of every subgroup H of G divides the order
of group G.
So, 15 is divided by {1, 3, 5, 15}.
As minimum order is 4 and not 15, so we eliminate 1,3,15.
So, the answer is 5.

questions.explanation:

questions.question: Let G be a connected planar graph with 10 vertices. If the number of edges on each face is three, then the number of edges in G is _______________. [ NAT || GATE 2015 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html: Let G be a connected planar graph with 10 vertices. If the number of edges on each face is three, then the number of edges in G is _______________.[ NAT || GATE 2015 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
By Euler’s formula,
We know that:
|V|+|R|-|E|= 2 ------(1) where |V|, |E|, |R| are respectively the number of vertices, edges and regions.
Given |V|=10 ------(2) and number of edges on each face is three.
∴3|R|=2|E|⇒|R|=2/3|E| ------(3)
Substituting (2), (3) in (1), we get
10+2/3|E|-|E|=2
⇒|E|/3=8
⇒|E|=24

In a room there are only two types of people, namely Type 1 and Type 2. Type 1 people always tell the truth and Type 2 people always lie. You give a fair coin to a person in that room, without knowing which type he is from and tell him to toss it and hide the result from you till you ask for it. Which of the following options is correct? [ MCQ || GATE 2015 Set-3 || 1 Marks ]

→ It is head and lies spoken.

Hence, option (A) is the correct answer.

questions.question: Let T be a tree with 10 vertices. The sum of the degrees of all the vertices in T is___________. [ NAT || GATE 2017 Set-1 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: Let T be a tree with 10 vertices. The sum of the degrees of all the vertices in T is___________. [ NAT || GATE 2017 Set-1 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
We know that a Tree T with n vertices has n-1 number of edges. So, Number of edges in T is : 10-1 = 9.
Now, by Handshaking Lemma, Sum of Degree of vertices is equal to twice the number of edges , so 2E = Sum of Degree.
Thus, Sum of degree = 2*9 = 18.

Thus, Sum of degree = 2*9 = 18.

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Graph Theory | Connected Component | PYQ | Question | 2018
questions.question:
Let G be a graph with 100! vertices, with each vertex labelled by a distinct permutation of the numbers 1, 2, …, 100.
There is an edge between vertices u and v if and only if the label of u can be obtained by swapping two adjacent numbers in the label of v. Let y denote the degree of a vertex in G and z denote the number of connected components in G. Then, y + 10z = ___________. [ NAT || GATE 2018 || 2 Marks]
questions.question_html:
Let G be a graph with 100! vertices, with each vertex labelled by a distinct permutation of the numbers 1, 2, …, 100.

There is an edge between vertices u and v if and only if the label of u can be obtained by swapping two adjacent numbers in the label of v. Let y denote the degree of a vertex in G and z denote the number of connected components in G. Then, y + 10z = ___________. [ NAT || GATE 2018 || 2 Marks]
questions.explanation:
G is a graph with 100! vertices. Label of each vertex is obtained from a distinct permutation of numbers “1, 2, … 100”.
There exists an edge between two vertices iff the label of ‘u’ is obtained by swapping two adjacent numbers in the label of ‘v’.
The sets of the swapping numbers be (1,2) (2, 3) (3, 4) … (99).
The no. of such sets are 99 i.e., no. of edges = 99.
As this is regular graph, each vertex has ‘99’ edges correspond to it.
So, degree of each vertex = 99 = y.
As the vertices are connected, the number of components formed = 1 = z
y + 10z = 99 + 10(1) = 109
questions.explanation_html:

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Mathematical Logics | Tautology | PYQ | Question  | 2016
questions.question:
Consider the following expressions:
(i) false
(ii) Q
(iii) true
(iv) P ∨ Q
(v) ¬Q ∨ P
The number of expressions given above that are logically implied by P ∧ (P ⇒ Q) is [ NAT || GATE 2016 Set-2 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Consider the following expressions:

(i) false

(ii) Q

(iii) true

(iv) P ∨ Q

(v) ¬Q ∨ P

The number of expressions given above that are logically implied by P ∧ (P ⇒ Q) is [ NAT || GATE 2016 Set-2 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
(P ∧ (P → Q)) = p ∧ (~p ∨ Q) = (p ∧ ~p) ∨(p ∧ Q) = p ∧ Q
(P ∧ Q) → True is always a tautology.
(P ∧ Q) → False is not a tautology
(P ∧ Q) → Q is a tautology
(P ∧ Q) →¬Q ∨ P is a tautology
(P ∧ Q) → P ∨ Q is a tautology
So, there are 4 expressions logically implied by (P ∧ (P → Q)).
questions.explanation_html:
(P ∧ (P → Q)) = p ∧ (~p ∨ Q) = (p ∧ ~p) ∨(p ∧ Q) = p ∧ Q

(P ∧ Q) → True is always a tautology.

(P ∧ Q) → False is not a tautology

(P ∧ Q) → Q is a tautology

(P ∧ Q) →¬Q ∨ P is a tautology

(P ∧ Q) → P ∨ Q is a tautology

So, there are 4 expressions logically implied by (P ∧ (P → Q)).

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Set Theory | Subset| PYQ | Question  | 2016
questions.question:
Consider a set U of 23 different compounds in a Chemistry lab. There is a subset S of U of 9 compounds, each of which reacts with exactly 3 compounds of U. Consider the
following statements:
I. Each compound in U\S reacts with an odd number of compounds.
II. At least one compound in U\S reacts with an odd number of compounds.
III. Each compound in U\S reacts with an even number of compounds.
Which one of the above statements is ALWAYS TRUE? [ MCQ || GATE 2016 Set-2 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Consider a set U of 23 different compounds in a Chemistry lab. There is a subset S of U of 9 compounds, each of which reacts with exactly 3 compounds of U. Consider the

following statements:

I. Each compound in U\S reacts with an odd number of compounds.

II. At least one compound in U\S reacts with an odd number of compounds.

III. Each compound in U\S reacts with an even number of compounds.

Which one of the above statements is ALWAYS TRUE? [ MCQ || GATE 2016 Set-2 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Given:
There is a set of ‘23’ different compounds.
U = 23
∃S ∋ (S⊂U)
Each component in ‘S’ reacts with exactly ‘3’ compounds of U,
Now,if a component ‘a’ reacts with ‘b’, then it is obvious that ‘b’ also reacts with ‘a’.
It’s a kind of symmetric relation. If we connect the reactable compounds, it will be an undirected graph.
The sum of the degree of vertices in S= 9 × 3 = 27.
number of vertices in U\S = 14
now if all vertices in U\S reacts with an odd number of compounds then the degree of all vertices in U\S would be even and the summation of degree of all vertices in U will be odd because odd+even=odd which is not possible because summation of all degree of vertices = 2 * number of edges = even
now if all vertices in U\S reacts with an even number of compounds then the degree of all vertices in U\S would be even and the summation of degree of all vertices in U will be odd because odd+even=odd which is again not possible .
hence At least one compound in U\S reacts with an odd number of compounds.

questions.question: The number of integers between 1 and 500 (both inclusive) that are divisible by 3 or 5 or 7 is ____________. [ NAT || GATE 2017 Set-1 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: The number of integers between 1 and 500 (both inclusive) that are divisible by 3 or 5 or 7 is ____________. [ NAT || GATE 2017 Set-1 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Let we have:
A → the number of integers between 1 and 500 which are divisible by 3.
B → the number of integers between 1 and 500 which are divisible by 5.
C → the number of integers between 1 and 500 which are divisible by 7.
Thus, the required answer is :
n (A ∪ B ∪ C) = n(A) + n(B) + n(C) – n (A∩ B) – n (A ∩ C) – n(B ∩ C) + n (A ∩ B ∩ C)
= [500/3] + [500/5] + [500/7] - [500/15] - [ 500/21] - [ 500/35] + [ 500/105]
= 166 + 100 + 71 – 33 – 23 – 14 + 4
= 271

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Set Theory | Countable Sets| PYQ | Question  | 2018
questions.question:
Let N be the set of natural numbers.
Consider the following sets,
P: Set of Rational numbers (positive and negative)
Q: Set of functions from {0, 1} to N
R: Set of functions from N to {0, 1}
S: Set of finite subsets of N.
Which of the above sets are countable? [MCQ || GATE 2018 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Let N be the set of natural numbers.

Consider the following sets,

P: Set of Rational numbers (positive and negative)

Q: Set of functions from {0, 1} to N

R: Set of functions from N to {0, 1}

S: Set of finite subsets of N.

Which of the above sets are countable? [MCQ || GATE 2018 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Set of rational numbers are countable. It is proved by various methods in literature.
Set of functions from {0,1} to N is countable since it has a one to one correspondence to set of natural numbers.
Set of functions from N to {0,1} is uncountable, as it has one to one correspondence to set of real numbers between (0 and 1).
Thus, Set of finite subsets of N is countable.
So, P, Q and S are countable.
questions.explanation_html:
Set of rational numbers are countable. It is proved by various methods in literature.

Set of functions from {0,1} to N is countable since it has a one to one correspondence to set of natural numbers.

Set of functions from N to {0,1} is uncountable, as it has one to one correspondence to set of real numbers between (0 and 1).

Thus, Set of finite subsets of N is countable.

So, P, Q and S are countable.

questions.question:
Let G be an arbitrary group. Consider the following relations on G:
R _ 1 : ∀a,b ∈ G, aR _1 b if and only if ∃g ∈ G such that a = g ^-1 bg
R _ 2 : ∀a,b ∈ G, aR _2 b if and only if a = b ^-1
Which of the above is/are equivalence relation/relations? [MCQ || GATE 2019 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Let G be an arbitrary group. Consider the following relations on G:

R 1 : ∀a,b ∈ G, aR1 b if and only if ∃g ∈ G such that a = g-1 bg

R 2 : ∀a,b ∈ G, aR2 b if and only if a = b -1

Which of the above is/are equivalence relation/relations? [MCQ || GATE 2019 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
A relation between the elements of a set if it satisfies symmetric, reflexive, and transitive property, then such relation is called as equivalence relation.
Consider Statement R _1 :
Reflexive:
aR _1 a
⇒ a = g ^-1 ag
By Left multiplying both sides by g:
⇒ga = gg ^ -1 ag
By Right multiplying both sides by g ^-1 :
⇒ gag ^-1  = gg ^-1 agg ^-1
⇒ gag ^ -1  = a [∴ The relation is reflexive]
Symmetric:
If aR _ 1 b, then ∃g ∈ G such that gag -1  = b
then a = g ^-1 bg, which is Correct.
⇒ So, given relation is symmetric.
Transitive:
The given relation is Transitive.
So, the given relation R _ 1  is equivalence.
R _2 :
The given relation is not reflexive. So, which is not equivalence relation. Such that a ≠ a ^-1 . So, only R 1  is true.

R2 :

questions.question: Let R be the set of all binary relations on the set {1,2,3}. Suppose a relation is chosen from R at random. The probability that the chosen relation is reflexive (round off to 3 decimal places) is _____. [NAT || GATE 2020 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: Let R be the set of all binary relations on the set {1,2,3}. Suppose a relation is chosen from R at random. The probability that the chosen relation is reflexive (round off to 3 decimal places) is _____. [NAT || GATE 2020 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
For a set with n elements,
The number of reflexive relations is 2 ^n(n-1) .
The total number of possible relations on a set with n elements is 2 ^ n*n .
Thus,the probability of choosing the reflexive relation out of the set of relations is:
= 2 ^ n(n-1) /2 ^n*n
= 2 ^(n*n – n*n – n) = 2 ^ (-n)
Given n=3, the probability will be
2 ^ -3 = = 0.125

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Combinatorics | Combinations | PYQ | Question | 2015
questions.question: The number of 4-digit numbers having their digits in non-decreasing order (from left to right) constructed by using the digits belonging to the set {1, 2, 3} is _____. [ NAT || GATE 2015 Set-3 || 1 Marks ]
questions.question_html: The number of 4-digit numbers having their digits in non-decreasing order (from left to right) constructed by using the digits belonging to the set {1, 2, 3} is _____. [ NAT || GATE 2015 Set-3 || 1 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
To form a four-digit number with non-decreasing digits from the set {1, 2, 3}, we can look at the possible combinations:
1. All digits are 1s: There's only one such number, 1111.
2. One digit is 2 and the rest are 1s: There are four such numbers, 1112, 1122, 1222, and 2222.
3. One digit is 3 and the rest are 1s: There are four such numbers, 1113, 1133, 1333, and 3333.
4. One digit is 2, one digit is 3, and the rest are 1s: There are six such numbers, 1123, 1133, 1223, 1233, 1333, and 2233.
Adding these up, we have a total of 1+4+4+6=15 four-digit numbers with non-decreasing digits from the set {1, 2, 3}.

Let p and q be two propositions. Consider the following two formulae in propositional logic.

questions.explanation:
S _1 : ¬p∧(p∨q)→q
If consequence is false and hypothesis is true, then we will get False in the truth table.
Lets assume q is false. So consequence is FALSE.
Can it make Hypothesis TRUE?
Hypothesis:
¬p∧(p∨q)≡¬p∧(p∨FALSE)≡¬p∧(p)≡FALSE.
Hypothesis can’t be true, So we can’t get False in the Truth Table.
∴ S1 is Tautology

∼q ∨ (∼p ∧ (p ∨ q))

∼ q ∨ (∼ p ∧ q)
(∼q ∨∼ p) ∧ T
it can be false when q=false and p=false
S _2 is not a tautology

questions.question:
Let G be a group of order 6, and H be subgroup of G such that 1 < |H| < 6.
Which one of the following options is correct? [MCQ || GATE 2021 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Let G be a group of order 6, and H be subgroup of G such that 1 < |H| < 6.

Which one of the following options is correct? [MCQ || GATE 2021 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]

options.text: G is always cyclic, but H may not be cyclic.
options.html: G is always cyclic, but H may not be cyclic.

options.text: G may not be cyclic, but H is always cyclic.
options.html: G may not be cyclic, but H is always cyclic.

options.text: Both G and H are always cyclic.
options.html: Both G and H are always cyclic.

options.text: Both G and H may not be cyclic.
options.html: Both G and H may not be cyclic.

questions.explanation:
As order of group is 6 (given) ,so according to Lagrange’s: order of subgroup is a divisor of order of group.
i.e., O(G) / O(H)
O(G) = 6 (not a prime no)
So, G is not a cyclic group.
Now, 1 < |H| < 6 so order of H may be 2,3, 4, 5 but only 2, 3 divides 6 so order of H is either 2 or 3 and both are prime number so H is cyclic group.

questions.question:
Let G(V, E) be a directed graph, where V = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5} is the set of vertices and E is the set of directed edges, as defined by the following adjacency matrix A.
A[i][j] = 1, 1 ≤ j ≤ i ≤ 5
A[i][j] = 0, otherwise
A[i][j] = 1 indicates a directed edge from node i to node j. A directed spanning tree of G, rooted at r ∈ V, is defined as a subgraph T of G such that the undirected version of T is a tree, and T contains a directed path from r to every other vertex in V. The number of such directed spanning trees rooted at vertex 5 is ____________. [ NAT || GATE 2022 || 2 Marks]

questions.explanation:
Counting by levels-
CASE 1) all 4 nodes on level 2 - 1 tree
CASE 2) any 3 nodes on level 2
We have nodes 1, 2, 3, 4
Out of them, select any 3 - ^4 C _3
Note that we cannot keep 1, 2, 3 on level 2 as there won't be any path to reach 4
Thus possible permutations -
1. (1, 2, 4) Only 1 possibility for 3, therefore - 1 tree
2. (1, 3, 4) 2 possibilities for 2 (child of 3 OR child of 4) - 2 trees
3. (2, 3, 4) 3 possibilities for 1 - 3 trees
Total trees in CASE 2 = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6
CASE 3) Any 2 nodes on level 2 Possible combinations
1. (1, 4)
1 possibility for 3
2 possibilities for 2
Thus, 1*2 = 2 trees
2. (2, 4)
1 possibility for 3
3 possibilities for 1
Thus, 1*3 = 3 trees
3. (3, 4)
2 possibilities for 2
3 possibilities for 1
Thus, 2*3 = 6 trees
Total trees in CASE 3 = 6 + 3 + 2 = 11
CASE 4) Only 1 node on level 2
Only possible node to keep on level 2 is 4
Thus,
3 has 1 possibility (child of 4)
2 has 2 possibilities (child of 3 OR child of 4)
1 has 3 possibilities (child of 3, child of 2, or child of 4)
Total trees in CASE 4 = 2*3 = 6
Total trees in all CASES = 1 + 6 + 11 + 6 = 24

questions.explanation:
Option : If for all x, y ∈ G, (xy)² = x²y², then G is commutative.
True,
(xy)² = x²y²
xy xy = xx yy
Take x⁻¹ and y⁻¹ on both sides.
x⁻¹ xy xy y⁻¹ = x⁻¹ xx yy y⁻¹
yx = xy
∀x, y ∈ G, yx = xy, Here G is commutative.
Option : If for all x ∈ G, x² = 1, then G is commutative. Here, 1 is the identity element of G.
True, ∀x ∈ G, x² = 1
If every element has its own inverse in a graph, then the graph is commutative.
Option : If the order of G is 2, then G is commutative.
True, Every group of prime order is commutative, so if O(G) = 2, the group 'G' is commutative. If the Group order is a prime number, then it is a cyclic group. And we know that every Cyclic group is an abelian group.
Alternatively, we can think like this: Given that the Order of Group G is 2, one element is the Identity element, so another element x is inverse to itself. By Option 3, G is commutative.
Option : If G is commutative, then a subgroup of G need not be commutative.
False, If group G is commutative, then a subgroup of G need not be commutative.
Theorem: Every subgroup of an abelian group has to be abelian.
Proof: Let G be an abelian group, and suppose that H ⊆ G.
Now we have to check that, for any a, b ∈ H, do we have ab = ba?
Since we have: a, b ∈ H ⊆ G, so a, b are elements of G and since G is abelian, so ab = ba.
Therefore, H is abelian as well.

questions.question:
Let G be a simple, finite, undirected graph with vertex set {v _1 ,...,v _n }.
Let Δ(G) denote the maximum degree of G and let N = {1, 2,...} denote the set of all possible colors. Color the vertices of G using the following greedy strategy:
for i = 1,...,n
color(v _i ) ← min {j ∈ N : no neighbour of v _i is colored j}
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? [ MSQ || GATE 2023 || 2 Marks]

Let Δ(G) denote the maximum degree of G and let N = {1, 2,...} denote the set of all possible colors. Color the vertices of G using the following greedy strategy:

for i = 1,...,n

color(vi) ← min {j ∈ N : no neighbour of vi is colored j}

questions.explanation:
Greedy means Assign the colour only if required otherwise use the existing assigned colours.
So here colour is getting assigned only if none of the neighbours have that colour and that assigning happening in greedy mode.
So it'll result in proper vertex colouring of G and also at max Chromatic Number will be <= 1+ Max Degree (There is a theorem on this.)

questions.question:
Let f : A → B be an onto (or surjective) function, where A and B are nonempty sets. Define an equivalence relation ∼ on the set A as
a _1 ∼ a _2 if f(a _1 ) = f(a _2 ) ,
where a _1 , a _2 ∈ A. Let ε = {[x] : x ∈ A} be the set of all the equivalence classes under ∼. Define a new mapping F : ε→ B as
F([x]) = f(x), for all the equivalence classes [x] in ε.
Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
[MSQ || GATE 2023 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Let f : A → B be an onto (or surjective) function, where A and B are nonempty sets. Define an equivalence relation ∼ on the set A as 

a1 ∼ a2 if f(a1) = f(a2) , 

where a1, a2 ∈ A. Let ε = {[x] : x ∈ A} be the set of all the equivalence classes under ∼. Define a new mapping F : ε→ B as 

F([x]) = f(x), for all the equivalence classes [x] in ε. 

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

[MSQ || GATE 2023 || 2 Marks ]

options.text: F is an onto (or surjective) function.
options.html: F is an onto (or surjective) function.

options.text: F is a one-to-one (or injective) function.
options.html: F is a one-to-one (or injective) function.

questions.explanation:
Here, equivalence relation ∼ on the set 𝐴 is defined as:
a _1 ∼a _2   if f(a _1 )=f(a _2 )
where a _1 , a _2 ∈A
So, consider ai,bi,ci,⋯∈A and the mapping as:
a _1 ↦α, a _2 ↦α, a _3 ↦α, …,a _m ↦α
Similarly,
b1↦β,b _2 ↦β,b _3 ↦β,…,b _n ↦β
c _1 ↦γ,c _2 ↦γ,c _3 ↦γ,…,c _p ↦γ
and so on for the non-empty sets A and B, and it is given that f:A→B is an onto function.
According to the definition of Equivalence relation, Equivalence class is:
[a _1 ]=[a _2 ]=[a _3 ]=⋯=[a _m ]
[b _1 ]=[b _2 ]=[b _3 ]=⋯=[b _n ]
[c _1 ]=[c _2 ]=[c _3 ]=⋯=[c _p ]
and so on.
Now, set of equivalence classes under relation ∼ is defined as:
E={[a _1 ],[b _1 ],[c _1 ],… }
Now, given the new mapping F:ε →B
F([x])=f(x) for all [x] ∈ ε
It means the mapping would be:
a _1 ↦α
b _1 ↦β
c _1 ​↦γ
and so on.
Since all distinct a _1 ,b _1 ,c _1 ,… maps to different elements of set B and so, F is an injective function. Here, we have taken a _1 ,b _1 ,c _1 as leaders for their own equivalence classes, you can take a _2 ,b _2 ,c _2 ,.... so on.
It is cleared from the mapping of F that all the elements of set B are covered, so F is a surjective function because each element from their own equivalence class maps according to the mapping of f. We have taken one element from the group of a′s and mapped it to a′s and similarly for other groups of b′s, c′s and so on.
Since F is both injective and surjective, hence F is a bijective function.
Here, we say, function F is well-defined or single-valued when:
- F ⊆ [x] × f(x)
- The domain of F is [x]
- If ([x],y),([x],z) then y=z
Since from the mapping of F, it is clear that all the above three conditions are true because we have taken one element from the group of a′s,b′s,c′s, and so on and mapped to α,β,γ,… and so f is a well-defined function.
Therefore, (B), (C), (D)

Here, equivalence relation ∼ on the set 𝐴 is defined as:

a1∼a2  if f(a1)=f(a2)

where a1, a2 ∈A

So, consider ai,bi,ci,⋯∈A and the mapping as:

a1↦α, a2↦α, a3↦α, …,am↦α

Similarly,

c1↦γ,c2↦γ,c3↦γ,…,cp↦γ

and so on for the non-empty sets A and B, and it is given that f:A→B is an onto function.

According to the definition of Equivalence relation, Equivalence class is:

[a1]=[a2]=[a3]=⋯=[am]

[b1]=[b2]=[b3]=⋯=[bn]

[c1]=[c2]=[c3]=⋯=[cp]

and so on.

Now, set of equivalence classes under relation ∼ is defined as:

Now, given the new mapping F:ε →B

F([x])=f(x) for all [x] ∈ ε

It means the mapping would be:

a1↦α

b1↦β

c1​↦γ

and so on.

Since all distinct a1,b1,c1,… maps to different elements of set B and so, F is an injective function. Here, we have taken a1,b1,c1 as leaders for their own equivalence classes, you can take a2,b2,c2,.... so on.

questions.question:
Let U={1,2,3}
Let 2 ^U denote the powerset of U.
Consider an undirected graph G whose vertex set is 2 ^U
For any A, B ∈2 ^U (A, B) is an edge in G if and only if:
- (i) A≠B and
- (ii) either A⊆B or B⊆A.
For any vertex A in G, the set of all possible orderings in which the vertices of G can be visited in a Breadth-First Search (BFS) starting from A is denoted by B(A).
If ϕ denotes the empty set, then the cardinality of B(ϕ) is? [NAT || GATE 2023 || 1 Marks ]

There is an edge between A and B vertices iff either of vertex set is proper subset to other vertex.
As we know ∅ is proper subset to every other set in the vertex set except itself.
That implies, ∅ is connected with 7 vertices directly, these 7 vertices can be visited in any order in BFS.
Therefore No.of BFS orders = 7! = 120x6x7 = 5040.

separates A from B if one of the following is true. All members of A appear in the permutation before any of the members of B.
All members of B appear in the permutation before any of the members of A.
How many permutations of U separate A from B? [ MCQ || GATE 2023 || 2 Marks ]

questions.explanation:
Instead of taking variables lets take a small example to first understand the question, once that’s done, the solutions would seem pretty intuitive!
Let n = 6
U = {1,2,3,4,5,6}
A = {1,2} and B = {4,5}
Here k = 2 and A ∩ B = Φ
Now, coming to the solution,
Step 1 : We first select 2k elements from n elements for creating A and B i.e ^n C _2k
Step 2 : Elements in A and B can permute amongst themselves respectively so k!×k!(i.e.,  ^n C _2k ×k!×k!)
Step 3 : Now there can be two cases – AB and BA i.e ^n C _2k ×k!×k!×2
Step 4 : The remaining elements (n-2k)! can arrange in any way so (n−2k)!
Thus, the expression becomes:
^n C _2k ×k!×k!×2×(n−2k)! ^n C _2k (n-2k)! ^n C _2k ×k!×k!×2×(n−2k)!
Thus, our solution becomes Option D.

questions.title: Discrete Mathematics | Mathematical Logics | Predicate Logics  PYQ | Question 2| 2025
questions.question:
Consider two relations describing teams and players in a sports league:
- teams(tid, tname): tid and tname are team-id and team-name, respectively.
- players(pid, pname, tid): pid, pname, and tid denote player-id, player-name, and the team-id of the player, respectively.
Which ONE of the following tuple relational calculus queries returns the name of the players who play for the team having tname as 'MI'? [ MCQ || GATE 2025 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Consider two relations describing teams and players in a sports league:

  • teams(tid, tname): tid and tname are team-id and team-name, respectively.
  • players(pid, pname, tid): pid, pname, and tid denote player-id, player-name, and the team-id of the player, respectively.


Which ONE of the following tuple relational calculus queries returns the name of the players who play for the team having tname as 'MI'? [ MCQ || GATE 2025 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]
questions.explanation:
Problem Understanding:
We have two relations:
- teams(tid, tname)  — stores team id and team name.
- players(pid, pname, tid)  — stores player id, player name, and the team id for each player.
We need a tuple relational calculus query that returns the name of the players who play for the team whose name is 'MI'. This means that for each player, we should find their team and check if the team name is 'MI'.
Query Breakdown: To retrieve the player names for the team "MI," we need:
- To find players in the  players  relation where the  tid  of the player matches the  tid  of a team in the  teams  relation.
- Additionally, we need to check that the  tname  of the team is 'MI'.
Now, let's analyze the options:
Option 1: { p.pname | p ∈ players ∧ ∃t (t ∈ teams ∧ p.tid = t.tid ∧ t.tname = 'MI') }
- This option checks that for every player  p  in the  players  relation, there exists a team  t  in the  teams  relation where the player's  tid  matches the team's  tid  and the team name is 'MI'.
- This query correctly finds the names of players whose team name is 'MI'.
Option 2: { p.pname | p ∈ teams ∧ ∃t (t ∈ players ∧ p.tid = t.tid ∧ t.tname = 'MI') }
- This option incorrectly uses  p ∈ teams , which means it is trying to retrieve players from the  teams  relation. This is incorrect because  teams  contains team data, not player data.
Option 3: { p.pname | p ∈ players ∧ ∃t (t ∈ teams ∧ t.tname = 'MI') }
- This option finds all players in the  players  relation but only checks if there exists any team with the name 'MI', without ensuring the player's team matches the team 'MI'. This query is not specific to the players whose team is 'MI'.
Option 4: { p.pname | p ∈ teams ∧ ∃t (t ∈ players ∧ t.tname = 'MI') }
- This option, like Option 2, incorrectly uses  p ∈ teams , which is not valid because we want players, not teams, to be queried.
Conclusion: The correct query is  Option 1 , as it correctly retrieves the player names whose team name is 'MI' based on the given conditions.
Answer: Option 1

questions.question_html: The chromatic number of a graph is the minimum number of colours used in a proper colouring of the graph. Let G be any graph with n vertices and chromatic number k. Which of the following statements is/are always TRUE? [ MSQ || GATE 2024 Set-1 || 2 Marks]

options.text: G contains a complete subgraph with k vertices.
options.html: G contains a complete subgraph with k vertices.

options.text: G contains an independent set of size at least n/k.
options.html: G contains an independent set of size at least n/k.

options.text: G contains at least k(k−1)/2 edges.
options.html: G contains at least k(k−1)/2 edges.

options.text: G contains a vertex of degree at least k.
options.html: G contains a vertex of degree at least k.

questions.explanation:
Option is false because chromatic number 'k' not necessarily means that graph contains a clique on 'k' vertices. For example, Chromatic Number of a Cycle Graph on odd vertices is 3 but it doesn't contain any complete subgraph on 3 vertices.
Option is True by virtue of Pigeon-Hole Principle. If there are 'k' color classes and n vertices, then one color class size is atleast n/k. Note that, a color class is nothing but an independent set.
Option is True because minimum number of edges between k color classes is C(K,2) or 'k choose 2'
Option is False because take a cycle graph on odd vertices, chromatic number is 3 but degree is not 3 for any vertex. Similarly, for any complete graph, degree for every vertex is n-1 but chromatic number is n

Consider the operators ⋄ and □ defined by:
- a⋄b=a+2b
- a□b=ab
for positive integers. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? [MSQ || GATE 2024 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]
questions.question_html:
Consider the operators ⋄ and □ defined by:

  • a⋄b=a+2b
  • a□b=ab

for positive integers. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE? [MSQ || GATE 2024 Set-1 || 2 Marks ]

options.text: Operator ⋄ over the operator □ obeys the distributive law.
options.html: Operator ⋄ over the operator □ obeys the distributive law.

options.text: Operator □ over the operator ⋄ obeys the distributive law.
options.html: Operator □ over the operator ⋄ obeys the distributive law.

questions.explanation:
An operation # is associative if (a#b)#c=a#(b#c).
Option A: Operation 1 follows Associativity? (a⋄b)⋄c=(a+2b)⋄c=a+2b+2c
a⋄(b⋄c)=a⋄(b+2c)=a+2b+4c
Here, (a⋄b)⋄c≠a⋄(b⋄c)
So, Option A is rejected.
Option B: Operation 2 follows Associativity? (a□b)□c=(ab)□c=abc
a□(b□c)=a□(bc)=abc
Here, (a□b)□c=a□(b□c)
So, Option B is Accepted.
NOTE : An operator % is distributive over @ if a % (b @ c) = (a % b) @ (a % c).
Option C: Operator ⋄ is distributive over □? Is: a⋄(b□c)=(a⋄b)□(a⋄c)
a⋄(b□c)=a⋄(bc)=a+2bc
(a⋄b)□(a⋄c)=(a+2b)□(a+2c)=a2+2ac+2ab+4bc
Here, a⋄(b□c)≠(a⋄b)□(a⋄c)
So, Option C is rejected.
Option D: Operator □ is distributive over ⋄? Is:
a□(b⋄c)=(a□b)⋄(a□c)?
a□(b⋄c)=a□(b+2c)=ab+2ac
(a□b)⋄(a□c)=(ab)⋄(ac)=ab+2ac
Here, a□(b⋄c)=(a□b)⋄(a□c)
So, Option D is Accepted.
questions.explanation_html:
An operation # is associative if (a#b)#c=a#(b#c).

Option A: Operation 1 follows Associativity?

(a⋄b)⋄c=(a+2b)⋄c=a+2b+2c

a⋄(b⋄c)=a⋄(b+2c)=a+2b+4c

Here, (a⋄b)⋄c≠a⋄(b⋄c)

So, Option A is rejected.

Option B: Operation 2 follows Associativity?

(a□b)□c=(ab)□c=abc

a□(b□c)=a□(bc)=abc

Here, (a□b)□c=a□(b□c)

So, Option B is Accepted.


NOTE : An operator % is distributive over @ if a % (b @ c) = (a % b) @ (a % c).

Option C: Operator ⋄ is distributive over □?

Is: a⋄(b□c)=(a⋄b)□(a⋄c)

a⋄(b□c)=a⋄(bc)=a+2bc

(a⋄b)□(a⋄c)=(a+2b)□(a+2c)=a2+2ac+2ab+4bc

Here, a⋄(b□c)≠(a⋄b)□(a⋄c)

So, Option C is rejected.

Option D: Operator □ is distributive over ⋄?

Is:

a□(b⋄c)=(a□b)⋄(a□c)?

a□(b⋄c)=a□(b+2c)=ab+2ac

(a□b)⋄(a□c)=(ab)⋄(ac)=ab+2ac

Here, a□(b⋄c)=(a□b)⋄(a□c)

So, Option D is Accepted.

(a,b,c)+(a,b,c)=(0,0,0)(modulo 2,3,4 respectively)
